mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar F-Pace SVR engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned F-Pace SVR engine typically costs £6,000–£9,500 supply only, with the 575 Edition variant at the higher end. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800 depending on the specialist. A documented warranty claim for a low-mileage SVR (27,020 miles) resulted in a £23,000 engine replacement bill. Reconditioned AJ133 engines with upgraded timing chain tensioners are available from specialist suppliers.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar F-Pace SVR engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The AJ133 is a masterpiece of power, but it's plagued by three well-documented flaws. The plastic coolant crossover pipes are the critical weakness — early two-piece pipes can crack catastrophically. Timing chain tensioner failure has given the SVR a notorious reputation, especially in 2019–2023 models. Supercharger coupler wear is also common. Later 2024 575 Edition cars received cast aluminium cooling pipes for improved durability. With proper maintenance and upgraded cooling pipes, the AJ133 can be a rewarding engine.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar F-Pace SVR?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most common issues are plastic coolant crossover pipe failure (cracked Y pipes or valley pipes in the V of the engine — causes sudden coolant loss and overheating), timing chain tensioner failure (especially 2019–2023 models — rattling on cold start is the warning sign), supercharger coupler wear (metallic rattling on cold start and clonk on shutdown), auxiliary radiator leaks, and oxygen sensor failures. Later 2024 575 Edition cars have upgraded cooling pipes.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an F-Pace SVR engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept facelift SVR worth £40,000–£60,000, a £7,000–£9,500 recon is 15–20% of the car's value — often worth it. Against a tired pre-facelift example worth £20,000–£30,000, the same recon may approach our 60% Jaguar threshold — get a scrap valuation first. The SVR's rarity and desirability mean engine replacement is often worthwhile for cherished examples.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Jaguar F-Pace SVR engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is AJ133, found on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation. It's the 5.0-litre supercharged V8 fitted to all F-Pace SVR models from 2018–2024. The P550 produces 550 PS; the 575 Edition produces 575 PS. Check the VIN plate or vehicle documentation to confirm which variant you have.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the AJ133 coolant crossover pipes, or do they need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The plastic pipes must be replaced — they are the weak point. Many owners upgrade to aftermarket aluminium pipes for a permanent fix. A preventative replacement costs £800–£1,500. Ignoring a coolant leak can lead to sudden overheating and catastrophic engine damage. At the first sign of coolant loss, book an inspection immediately. Later 2024 575 Edition cars have upgraded cast aluminium pipes as standard.

replacementCosts.h2: Jaguar F-Pace SVR Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current F-Pace SVR replacement costs. The 575 Edition variant typically sits at the higher end of the range. See the Era Map above for generation-specific pricing.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an F-Pace SVR engine typically takes 16–22 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). Average UK labour rates have risen from £65.63 to £68.51 per hour.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: F-Pace SVR — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential maintenance — upgrade to aluminium pipes
Only if caught early; often more cost-effective to replace the engine
Essential maintenance — catch it before catastrophic failure
Piston-valve contact means little is left to repair
Catch it early — far cheaper than supercharger replacement
Oil leaks from nose seal contaminate belts

buyingChecks.title: Buying This F-Pace SVR? Check These First
buyIf: * Post-2021 facelift with updated cooling system — revised pipes and lighter internals
* 2024 575 Edition with cast aluminium cooling pipes
* Coolant pipes upgraded to aftermarket aluminium replacements
* Full service history with frequent oil changes — do not rely on JLR's 16,000-mile interval
* No cold-start rattling (timing chain and supercharger health)
* Auxiliary radiators checked for leaks (known issue with mounting brackets)
avoidIf: * Pre-2018 models with original plastic coolant pipes and no history of replacement
* Cold-start rattling — timing chain tensioner or supercharger coupler may be failing
* No service history or evidence of extended oil change intervals — the AJ133 is unforgiving of neglect
* Any history of overheating or coolant loss — cooling system damage may have occurred
* Oil consumption exceeding 1L per 1,000 miles — may indicate bottom-end wear

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The F-Pace SVR is Jaguar's fire-breathing supercharged V8 SUV — the highest-selling Jaguar SV product ever, powered by the 5.0-litre AJ133. The engine is a masterpiece of power, delivering 550–575 PS and up to 700 Nm torque, but it's plagued by three well-documented flaws that UK owners know all too well: plastic coolant crossover pipes that can crack catastrophically, timing chain tensioner failure (especially in 2019–2023 models), and supercharger coupler wear. Later 2024 575 Edition cars received cast aluminium cooling pipes for improved durability. The F-Pace SVR has a smaller oil pan than Range Rover products, so more frequent oil changes are essential. With typical SVR values ranging from £28,000 (high-mileage pre-facelift) to £78,000+ (low-mileage facelift), an engine replacement is a major financial commitment. A well-maintained 2021-on facelift or 575 Edition with documented frequent oil changes and upgraded cooling system pipes is the safest choice.

cta.label: → Compare F-Pace SVR engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Jaguar F-Pace SVR (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Coolant System — Check for leaks around the supercharger area; inspect coolant crossover pipes for cracks or signs of failure — see the map above
* Cold Start — Listen for metallic rattling (supercharger coupler) or rough running (timing chain tensioner)
* Coolant Level — Check reservoir when cold; any low or loss may indicate pipe, water pump or radiator issues
* Service History — Including which generation (pre-facelift, facelift or 575 Edition) and oil-change frequency
* Oil Consumption — Ask about oil top-up frequency; AJ133 engines can burn 1L per 1,000 miles
* Supercharger Health — Listen for "clonk" when shutting down (coupler wear)
* Auxiliary Radiators — Check for leaks (known issue with mounting brackets on early cars)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate timing, cooling or sensor problems (O2 sensors are common)
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
